# MAIN ARTICLE

www.acsami.org

Research Article


# An Ultrasmall RuO2Nanozyme Exhibiting Multienzyme-like Activity for the Prevention of Acute Kidney Injury


Zhou Liu, Lina Xie, Kangqiang Qiu, Xinxing Liao, Thomas W. Rees, Zizhuo Zhao, * Liangnian Ji, and Hui Chao *

Cite This: https://dx.doi.org/10.1021/acsami.0c07886

ABSTRACT: Oxidative stress induced by reactive oxygen species (ROS) is one of the major pathological mechanisms of acute kidney injury (AKI). Inorganic nanomaterial-mediated antioxidant therapy is considered a promising method for the prevention of AKI; however, currently available antioxidants for AKI exhibit limited clinical efficacy due to the glomerularfiltration threshold ( ∼ 6nm). To address this issue, we developed ultrasmall RuO2nanoparticles (RuO2NPs) (average size ≈ 2nm). The NPs show excellent antioxidant activity and low biological toxicity. In addition, they can pass through the glomerulus to be excreted. These properties in combination make the ultrasmall RuO2NPs promising as a nanozyme for the prevention of AKI. The NP catalytic properties mimic the activity of catalase, peroxidase, superoxide dismutase, and glutathione peroxidase. The nanozyme can be efficiently and rapidly absorbed by human embryonic kidney cells while significantly reducing ROS-induced apoptosis by eliminating excess ROS. After intravenous injection, the ultrasmall RuO2NPs significantly inhibit the development of AKI in mice. In vivo toxicity experiments demonstrate the biosafety of the NPs after long-term preventing. The multienzyme-like activity and biocompatibility of the ultrasmall RuO2NPs makes them of great interest for applications in thefields of biomedicine and biocatalysis.

KEYWORDS:

RuO2nanoparticles, multienzyme-like activity, ultrasmall, acute kidney injury, in vivo clearance


# 1. INTRODUCTION


Reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as singlet oxygen ( 1O2), superoxide anions (O2 · -), hydroxyl radicals ( · OH), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), are the metabolites of normal cells in living organisms. 1 -3Moderate levels of ROS are important for maintaining cellular signal transduction and other intracellular functions. 4 -6Excess ROS may, however, induce oxidative stress in normal cells, which can, in turn, trigger a series of harmful processes in normal cells, including cell death and inflammation. 7,8Acute kidney injury (AKI) is a clinical syndrome with a high lethality that can result from one or more etiologies. It is also one of the more common disorders considered ' critical illnesses ' . 9,10AKI usually manifests as kidney damage leading to renal insufficiency, which results in the buildup of metabolic waste and perturbation of water, electrolyte, and acid -base homeostasis. 11Numerous studies have confirmed that oxidative stress, apoptosis, and inflam-

matory responses play important roles in the pathogenesis of AKI. Oxidative stress contributes toward AKI and typically involves the overproduction of ROS. 12 -15Patients with AKI have excessive oxidative stress due to increased oxide activity and decreased antioxidant activity in their serum, which subsequently leads to renal dysfunction. 16

Eliminating excess ROS can protect tissues from oxidative stress and effectively scavenging excess ROS can, therefore, have a significant therapeutic effect on AKI. 17,18Under pathological conditions, natural enzymes, such as peroxidase

Received:

April29, 2020

Accepted:

June22, 2020

Scheme1. Schematic Representation of an Ultrasmall RuO2Nanozyme for the Prevention of Acute Kidney Injury

[POD], superoxide dismutase [SOD], catalase [CAT], and glutathione peroxidase [GPx]), which help maintain cellular redox balance, are sensitive to the physiological environment and prone to inactivation. 19 -21NPs with enzyme-like activities have recently become the focus of research to solve this problem, because of their excellent physiochemical properties, biological safety, biostability, and ROS scavenging ability. 22 -25A large number of enzyme-like nanomaterials, including metal oxides, 26 -29metal nanomaterials, 30,31and polymers, 32have been used innovatively to treat diseases caused by ROS. However, because of the glomerularfiltration threshold ( ∼ 6nm), 33 -35most large-sized nanozymes are poorly absorbed by the kidneys, leading to potential organ toxicity. 36 -38This limits the use of large-sized nanozymes for the treatment of AKI diseases. Ultrasmall NPs, whose hydration diameter is lower than the kidneyfiltration threshold, can be rapidly excreted through the glomeruli. 39 -42Ultrasmall nanozymes that have excellent antioxidant activity and low biological toxicity and can be excreted by the kidney are highly desirable as potential therapeutic agents for the prevention of AKI. Their development, however, remains a challenge.

We herein report the synthesis of an ultrasmall RuO2nanozyme with good biocompatibility and enzyme-like activity for the prevention of AKI. As illustrated in Scheme1, the ultrasmall RuO2NPs can be used as an antioxidant because of their multienzyme-like (CAT, GPx, and SOD) properties. In vitro results demonstrate that the ultrasmall NPs have a remarkable cytoprotective effect on oxidative stress-induced nephrotoxicity. An AKI mouse model was used to elucidate the in vivo therapeutic properties of the ultrasmall RuO2nanozyme. Because of their ultrasmall size, effective renal accumulation, renal clearance, and long-term biosafety of the NPs were demonstrated in vivo. The excellent properties of these ultrasmall RuO2NPs highlight their promise as a multienzyme-like nanozyme for the prevention of AKI.


# 2. MATERIALS AND METHODS


Ruthenium(III) chloride trihydrate (RuCl3 · 3H2O), salicylic acid (SA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), FeSO4, and peroxy orange1 (PO) were purchased from Aladdin. Chitosan (CS), thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were obtained from Energy Chemical. Glycerinum was purchased from Macklin. Acetic acid, 3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), 2 ′ ,7 ′ -dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA), ethanol, 5,5-dimethyl-1pyrrolineN -oxide (DMPO), and an Annexin V-FITC apoptosis detection kit were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Antigamma H2A.X antibodies were acquired from Abcam Co. Ltd. A mitochondrial membrane potential assay kit was purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology. Malondialdehyde (MDA) assay, creatine kinase assay, urea assay, and creatinine (Cr) assay kits were obtained from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute. Superoxide dismutase (SOD) assay and glutathione peroxidase (GSH-PX) assay kits were purchased from Suzhou Comin Biotechnology.

2.1. Characterization. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained on a JEOL JEM-ARM200P microscope. The elemental concentration of Ru was measured by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, Thermo Fisher Scientific iCAP RQ). A Malvern Zetasizer Nanoseries was applied to record the hydrodynamic particle size. A LSM810NLO, (Carl Zeiss AG) confocal laser scanning microscope was used for cell imaging. UV -vis absorption spectroscopy was determined on a RF-5301spectrometer. The imaging of H&E-stained sections was performed on a positivefluorescence microscope (Carl Zeiss AG, AX10). A Milli-Q system by Millipore was used to obtain ultrapure water. A Bruker ELEXSYS E580X-band pulse electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer was utilized to detect free radicals. Cytotoxicity was determined by an MTT assay using a light absorption microplate reader (Molecular Devices, Spectra Max). Quantitative cell analysis was obtained byflow cytometer (FACS Canto II, BD Biosciences).

2.2. Synthesis of RuO2NPs. RuO2NPs were synthesized by the solvothermal method. Three milligrams per milliliter CS was dissolved in3% acetic acid. RuCl3 · 3H2O (25mg) was added to the CS solution (3mg/mL, 20mL), and stirred for30min before adding to a round-bottomedflask. The mixed solution was then heated to120 ° C for6h. A dark green solution was obtained and purified by dialysis with distilled water at room temperature for48h (MWCO: 3500).

2.3. Antioxidative Enzyme-like Properties of RuO2NPs. The CAT-like activity of RuO2NPs was measured by recording the changes in the absorption peak of H2O2at240nm on a UV -vis spectrophotometer. In the assay, RuO2NPs (20 μ g/mL) was added to the solution of H2O2 (1mM), and the elimination rate (decrease of H2O2) was measured over5min at room temperature.

The POD-like activity of RuO2NPs was investigated by using TMB as the substrate. TMB (1mM), H2O2 (1mM), and RuO2NPs ( 20 μ g/mL) were combined at room temperature for5min in an acetate buffer solution. The absorbance of TMB (650nm) was recorded after30min of reaction time.

The ability of RuO2NPs to quench the superoxide anion (O2 · -) was determined by measuring the formation of formazan using a SOD assay kit. O2 · -was produced through the oxidation of xanthine by xanthine oxidase. O2 · -reacts with azetidine to produce the watersoluble dye formazan. The formazan concentration was determined by measuring absorbance at560nm.

The GSH-Px-like activity of RuO2NPs was evaluated using a GSH-Px assay kit. GSH-Px catalyzes the oxidation of glutathione (GSH) by hydrogen peroxide which produces oxidized glutathione (GSSG). Glutathione reductase (GR) catalyzes the reaction of GSSG with NADPH, regenerating GSH and oxidizing NADPH to NADP+. NADPH has a characteristic absorption peak at340nm, whereas NADP+ does not. GSH-Px activity was therefore calculated by recording the rate of decrease in light absorption at340nm.

- 2.4. Steady-State Kinetic Assays. The catalytic parameters were determined by converting the absorbance to the concentration of substrate and then the Michaelis -Menten equation wasfitted, 43which described the relationship between the conversion rate of the enzyme ( RuO2NPs ) to the substrate and the concentration of substrate. The formula was shown in the following:

In this formula, V represented the conversion rate, Vmax indicated the maximum conversion rate, Km was the Michaelis constant, and [S] was the concentration of substrate.

- The · OH-scavenging activity of RuO2NPs was also assessed byfluorescence spectroscopy. · OH was produced by the reaction between FeSO4 (1mM) and H2O2 (2mM) for5min. The · OH scavenging activity of the NPs was determined by recording the characteristic absorbance at510nm, which was generated by the reaction of SA (1.5mM) with · OH.

- 2.5. Hydroxyl Radical ( · OH) Scavenging Activity of the RuO2NPs. EPR spectroscopy was used to evaluate the ability of the RuO2NPs to scavenge ( · OH). The trapping agent DMPO was utilized to monitor the reaction of the NPs with · OH. DMPO/ · OH yields a characteristic EPR signal with a peak ratio of1:2:2:1. The · OH-scavenging ability of RuO2NPs was recorded by incubating H2O2 (1mM), in the presence of FeSO4 (10 μ M), and following by the addition of RuO2NPs (20 μ g/mL) and DMPO (50mM). The EPR signal was measured after5min of reaction.

- 2.6. Cell Culture. All cells were cultured in a humidified atmosphere at37 ° C, 5% CO2, and95% air. Human embryonic kidney cells (HEK293) were incubated in DMEM with10% fetal calf serum and1% penicillin streptomycin.

- 2.7. Cytotoxity Assay. The cell viability was calculated by MTT assay. HEK293cells were seeded into a96-well plate and grown overnight. H2O2, RuO2NPs , or RuO2NPs +H2O2 (H2O2: 500 μ M, RuO2NPs : 20 μ g/mL) were added to the wells, and the cells then cultured for24h. At same time, 1 μ M Calcein-AM and2 μ M propidium iodide (PI) were also applied to investigate the viability of cells (Calcein AM for live cells; PI for dead cells). Thefluorescence images were taken on an invertedfluorescence microscope.

- 2.8. H2O2Production in HEK293Cells. HEK293cells seeded in a96-well plate were incubated with H2O2, RuO2NPs , or RuO2NPs +H2O2 (H2O2: 500 μ M, RuO2NPs : 20 μ g/mL). After24h of incubation, the cells were washed with PBS for three times before2 μ M peroxy orange I was added into the cells, which then were cultured for a further30min. After a further wash by PBS, thefluorescence intensity was recorded using a multiplate reader ( λ ex = 548nm, λ em = 610nm).

- 2.9. Detection of Intracellular ROS. Intracellular ROS measurement was assayed by2 ′ ,7 ′ -dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA) staining. HEK293cells were incubated in confocal microscope dishes for24h, and the cell density was1 × 10 5cells/ well. After incubation, the cells were incubated with H2O2, RuO2NPs , or RuO2NPs +H2O2 (H2O2: 500 μ M, RuO2NPs : 20 μ g/mL) for a further24h. The cells were then washed three times with PBS and cultured with DCFH-DA (5 μ M) at37 ° C for a further30min. The intracellular ROS levels were determined by confocal laser scanning microscopy (Carl Zeiss AG) ( λ ex = 480nm, λ em = 525nm).

- 2.10. Mitochondrial Membrane Potential (MMP) Assay. Mitochondrial membrane potential perturbation was measured with a JC-1mitochondrial membrane potential assay kit. HEK293cells were

- incubated in a6-well plate for24h, the cells density was2 × 10 5cells/well and cultured with H2O2, RuO2NPs , or RuO2NPs +H2O2 (H2O2: 500 μ M, RuO2NPs : 20 μ g/mL) for a further24h. And then, the cells were trypsinized and collected, then stained with JC-1following the manufacture ' s protocol. The cells obtained were then investigated byflow cytometry (BD FACS Canto II).

- 2.11. Detection of Cell Apoptosis. The apoptosis of HEK293cells was assessed by the Annexin V Apoptosis Detection Kit (SigmaAldrich). HEK293cells were incubated in a6-well plate for24h, the cells density was2 × 10 5cells/well and cultured with H2O2, RuO2NPs , or RuO2NPs +H2O2 (H2O2: 500 μ M, RuO2NPs : 20 μ g/ mL) After treatment, the cells were collected and then washed three times with ice-cold PBS before staining with Annexin V and PI. The collected cells were then assessed byflow cytometry.

- 2.12. DNA Double-Strand Breaks. HEK293cells were incubated in confocal microscope dishes for24h, the cells density was1 × 10 5cells/well. After incubation, the cells were incubated with H2O2, RuO2NPs , or RuO2NPs +H2O2 (H2O2: 500 μ M, RuO2NPs : 20 μ g/mL) for a further24h. The cells were washed three times with PBS andfixed with4% paraformaldehyde for15min, and permeabilized in0.2% Triton-100for10min. After blocked (1% bovine serum albumin) for1h, antiγ H2AX antibodies were incubated withfixed cells overnight at4 ° C. Cells were washed by PBS and then cultured with secondary antibodies for1h at room temperature. All cells were counterstained with Hoechst. Immunofl uorescence was recorded by confocal laser scanning microscopy.

- 2.13. Establishment of an Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Model. All female, ICR mice (4 -6weeks old, Beijing Vital River Laboratory Technology Co., Ltd.) were randomly divided intofive groups: (i) injected with PBS only; (ii) injected with RuO2NPs only; (iii) injected with glycerol only; (iv) injected with glycerol and PBS; and (v) injected with glycerol and RuO2NPs . Groups iii, iv, and v were then provided with food but no water for12h. After the water restriction period ended, group iv was intravenously injected with PBS and group v with RuO2NPs (5mg/kg). Two hours postinjection, both hind limbs of groups iii, iv, and v received intramuscular injection of50% glycerol (8mL/kg) to trigger AKI, and all mice were then free to obtain food and water. The AKI model was established successfully and used for the following studies.

- 2.14. Renal Clearance Analysis. RuO2NPs (5mg/kg) were intravenously injected into healthy mice. Subsequently, urine and feces were collected at various time (4, 8, and12h and1, 2, 3, 5, 6, and7days). The obtained samples were dissolved in80% nitric acid and the amount of Ru was analyzed by ICP-MS. The urine samples from0, 4, 12, and24h time points were also examined by TEM imaging to further evaluate the renal clearance of the RuO2NPs .

- 2.15. Biodistribution Analysis. RuO2NPs (5mg/kg) were intravenously injected into healthy mice. The mice were sacrificed at various time (2, 4, 8, 12, 24, 48, and72h and7days) postinjection. The organs were collected and weighed for the quantitative analysis of the biodistribution of RuO2NPs . The obtained organs were dissolved in80% nitric acid, and Ru accumulation in the organs was evaluated by ICP-MS.

- 2.16. Therapeutic Effect in AKI Mice. To analyze the therapeutic effect of RuO2NPs , we divided the mice intofive treatment groups: (i) healthy mice treated with PBS; (ii) healthy mice treated with RuO2NPs (5mg/kg); (iii) AKI mice without further treatment; (iv) AKI mice treated with PBS; (v) AKI mice treated with RuO2NPs (5mg/kg). After treatment, kidney function and body weight were monitored over time.

- 2.17. Evaluation of the Effect of Treatment on AKI Status. Biomarkers of kidney function in AKI mice were evaluated by hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining in order to investigate the prevention effect of the RuO2NPs on AKI. All the groups were euthanized at different time ' s postinjection, (1, 2, 3, and7days). Their blood samples were collected and centrifuged at1500rpm for20min at4 ° C to obtain plasma. The levels of blood urea nitrogen (BUN), creatine (CK), malondialdehyde (MDA), and creatinine (CRE) were determined using an ELSA Kit.

Figure1. Multienzyme-like activity of the RuO2NPs . (A) CAT-like activity measured by H2O2elimination over time ( n = 3). (B) POD-like activity measured by the change in absorbance of TMB under four conditions: (i) TMB + RuO2NPs , (ii) RuO2NPs + H2O2, (iii) TMB+ H2O2, and (iv) TMB + RuO2NPs + H2O2. (C) SOD-like activity of RuO2NPs with concentration (2.5 -20 μ g/mL). (D) GSH-Px-like activity of RuO2NPs with concentration (10 -50 μ g/mL) ( n = 3).

H&E staining analysis was conducted on kidneys from all groups. Kidneys were collected from all groups after24h of treatment andfixed with4% paraformaldehyde. All tissues were then subsequently sectioned and stained with H&E. Finally, the tissue structures were observed with a positivefluorescence microscope (Carl Zeiss AG, AX10)

To analyze ROS production histologically, the kidneys from all groups were harvested and stored at -20 ° C and subsequently sectioned into tissue slices. The obtained tissue slices were washed three times with PBS and then costained with DCFH-DA and Hochest to measure ROS production. Subsequently, a cover glass was added to each slide, and the slides were imaged by confocal microscopy.

2.18. In Vivo Toxicity Assessment. Healthy mice were randomized into three groups. Healthy mice were injected with PBS intravenously as a control. Intravenous injection of RuO2NPs (5mg/kg) in healthy mice as the second group. The third group consist of AKI mice injected intravenously with RuO2NPs (5mg/kg). The mice from all groups were sacrificed24h postinjection. Histological changes in the organs of the mice were assessed by H&E staining. The glomeruli, collecting ducts, tubules, and urethrae from the second group of mice intravenously injected with RuO2NPs were also collected and stained with H&E to evaluate the renal toxicity of the RuO2NPs .

2.19. Ethics Statement. This study was performed with the approval of the Experimental Animal Manage Committee (EAMC) of Sun Yat-Sen University. Animals were treated as called for in the guidelines of EAMC.


# 3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION


3.1. Characterization of the RuO2NPs. The RuO2NPs were prepared via our previously reported method, 44which is both convenient and reproducible. Analysis of the as-obtained RuO2NPs was performed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Figure S1A, B) which revealed that the mean diameter of the RuO2NPs was ∼ 2nm, and the hydrodynamic size of RuO2NPs was about5.4nm (Figure S1C). And the

Brunauer -Emmett -Teller surface area was calculated to be14.46m2g -1 (Figure S1D). As shown in Figure S1A inset, the RuO2NPs in PBS exhibit a uniform green color, demonstrating the excellent dispersion of the NPs.

3.2. Multienzyme-like Activity of the RuO2NPs. The multienzyme-like activity of RuO2NPs was evaluated. The CAT-like activity of the RuO2NPs was investigated by recording the changes in absorbance of H2O2. As shown in Figure1A, the CAT-like activity of the RuO2NPs showed good results within5min. CAT can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2into H2O and O2, thereby protecting cells and tissues from oxidative damage. 45A rapid decomposition of H2O2was observed within5min. The decomposition of H2O2can also be observed qualitatively as the formation of bubbles (Figure S1E). A steady-state kinetic analysis was applied to measure the CAT-like activity of the RuO2NPs (Figure S2). And the Michaelis -Menten equation was introduced to obtain typical curve within a suitable concentration range of H2O2. This reaction indicates typical Michaelis -Menten kinetic. Km represents the affinity of the enzyme for the substrate, and a lower Km value indicates higher affinity. 43The Km value of the RuO2NPs with H2O2as the substrate was3.59mM, and the Vmax value was2.29mM/ min. Overall, these results illustrate that RuO2NPs act as an efficient CAT-like agent.

Using3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as the chromogenic substrate, the peroxidase (POD)-like activity of the RuO2NPs was detected (Figure1B). TMB can acts as a hydrogen donor for H2O2reduction, and the resultant diamine has a characteristic absorption peak at650nm. After10min reaction, the experimental group (TMB + RuO2NPs + H2O2) exhibited a clear absorption characteristic peak at650nm, and the solution turned blue. As shown in Figure S3, a steady-state kinetic analysis, with TMB and H2O2as the substrates, was further used to evaluate the POD-like

Figure2. (A) Intracellular H2O2scavenging ability of RuO2NPs in HEK293cells ( n = 3). (B) Cell viability of HEK293cells ( n = 3). (C) Fluorescence images of calcein AM and PI-stained HEK293cells. Control: untreated; RuO2NPs , 20 μ g/mL; H2O2, 500 μ M. Scale bar: 100 μ m.

Figure3. Production of intracellular ROS in HEK293cells untreated (control), treated with H2O2 (500 μ M), or RuO2NPs (20 μ g/mL) and H2O2 (500 μ M). Scale bar: 10 μ m.

activity of the RuO2NPs . The Km value of the RuO2NPs with TMB as the substrate was0.0990mM, and the Vmax value was12.9 × 10 -6M/min. The Km value of the RuO2NPs with

H2O2as the substrate was0.146mM, and the Vmax value was12.4 × 10 -6M/min, indicating that RuO2NPs exhibited excellent POD-like catalytic activity.

Figure4. Flow cytometry of JC-1-stained HEK293cells untreated (control) or treated with H2O2 (500 μ M), RuO2NPs (20 μ g/mL), or RuO2NPs (20 μ g/mL) and H2O2 (500 μ M).

SOD-like enzymes can be used to treat a variety of oxidative stress-related diseases. 46,47Expect for their CAT and POD-like activity, the RuO2NPs also showed SOD-like property. The SOD-like ability of the RuO2NPs to eliminate O2 ·-was measured using formazan, produced by the reaction of O2 · -and nitrogen blue tetrazole. The specific absorption of formazan was measured at560nm. At different concentrations of RuO2NPs , the absorption of formazan was obviously reduced, indicating the SOD-like activity of the NPs (Figure1C).

GSH-Px catalyzes the oxidation of GSH to GSSG to promote the elimination of H2O2. 48GSH react with dinitrobenzoic acid to form the5-thio-dinitrobenzoic acid anion with a characteristic absorption at412nm. The concentration of the anion was determined by measuring the absorbance at412nm to evaluate the GSH-Px-like activity of the RuO2NPs , and therefore indirectly estimate GSH concentration. As shown in Figure1D, an increased GSHPx-like activity was observed with increased concentration. By changing the various concentration of H2O2and GSH, steadystate kinetic measurements were used to confirm the enzyme catalytic mechanism. This reactionfitted well with the typical Michaelis -Menten kinetic (Figure S4), and the Km value of the RuO2NPs with GSH as the substrate was0.870mM, and the Vmax value was65.1 μ M/min. The Km value of the RuO2NPs with H2O2as the substrate was17.2 μ M, and the Vmax value was47.0 μ M/min. These results demonstrated good GSH-Px-like activity and efficiency of RuO2NPs .

The ability of RuO2NPs to scavenge · OH was determined by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy and UV -visible absorption spectroscopy. 49The signal intensity of the trapping agent5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO)/ · OH was diminished in the presence of RuO2NPs (Figure S5A). In addition, The · OH-scavenging activity45was assessed by measuring the absorbance of2,3-dihydroxybenzoic acid (the product of reaction between salicylic acid (SA) and · OH) at510nm. As shown in Figure S5B, in the presence of RuO2NPs , the absorption peak at510nm was significantly reduced, clearly demonstrating the · OH-scavenging activity. Overall, these results show that not only do RuO2NPs have multienzyme-like activity but they can also effectively scavenge · OH. The above results display the excellent potential of RuO2NPs as therapeutic agents that can restrain oxidative stress and reduce ROS-induced damage.

3.3. Preventing Cell Death by Efficient ROS Scavenging. H2O2reacts to generate free radicals responsible for the cause of cell damage. 50The ability of the RuO2NPs to reduce the levels of H2O2and ROS production was therefore investigated in HEK293cells. As shown in Figure2A, peroxy orange I (PO) was utilized as a H2O2-sensingfluorescent probe to evaluate the behavior of the NPs to reduce H2O2. HEK293cells treated with RuO2NPs showed lower intracellular H2O2production than H2O2-treated cells. The cell viability of H2O2-treated and RuO2NPs /H2O2-treated cells was subsequently studied. As shown in Figure2B, RuO2NPs treatment can significantly improve the cell viability caused by excessive ROS generated by H2O2. The live -dead cell staining result further verified the admirable biological safety and prophylactive properties of RuO2NPs (Figure2C).

DCFH-DA was used to detect intracellular ROS to further verify the ability of RuO2NPs to limit H2O2induced ROS production in HEK293cells. As shown in Figure3, it demonstrate that H2O2treatment of HEK293cells stimulates ROS production, while in RuO2NPs /H2O2-treated cells reduced ROS production was observed. These results indicate that RuO2NPs can effectively reduce the generation of free radicals mediated by H2O2. Moreover, RuO2NPs are biosafe for in vitro and in vivo applications.

3.4. Inhibition of Apoptosis by RuO2NPs in Cells under Oxidative Stress. Excessive ROS can induce cell apoptosis. Because renal tubules are vulnerable to oxidative stress, HEK293cells were used to evaluate the ability of the RuO2NPs to protect against excessive ROS. An Annexin V/PI apoptosis asssay was conducted to investigate HEK293cell death in cells treated with RuO2NPs and H2O2 (Figure S6). Apoptotic cells accounted for6.04% in the control group. In the presence of RuO2NPs alone, 14.6% of cells underwent apoptosis, whereas exposure to500 μ M H2O2induced apoptosis in42.9% of cells. In the group treated with both H2O2and RuO2NPs , the rate of apoptosis was significantly reduced (19.1%). This experiment provides convincing evidence that RuO2NPs can protect cells against oxidative stress.

3.5. Prevention of Mitochondrial Injury and DNA Damage by RuO2NPs. Excessive ROS is known to cause mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) loss, which is an important sign of mitochondrial dysfunction. 51Flow cytometry was used to assess HEK293cells stained with JC-1to characterize whether RuO2NPs protect against oxidative stress induced by H2O2. The results show that the MMP of the untreated control cells was normal. After addition of RuO2NPs , no significant change in MMP was observed. The MMP decreased after exposure to H2O2, which is regarded as an early and irreversible step of apoptosis, whereas cells treated with both H2O2and RuO2NPs gave very similar results to cells treated with the NPs alone (Figure4). These

Figure5. Laser confocal scanning microscope imaging of γ -H2AX after no treatment (control) or treatment with either H2O2 (500 μ M) or RuO2NPs (20 μ g/mL) and H2O2 (500 μ M). γ -H2AX shows as redfluorescence, and cell nuclei stained by Hoechst show as bluefluorescence. Scale bar: 10 μ m.

Figure6. (A) Pharmacokinetics ( n = 3) and (B) biodistribution of RuO2NPs in AKI mice in kidney at various time after intravenous injection ( n = 3). (C) Amounts of Ru in urine and feces collected from AKI mice at various time intervals after intravenous injection with RuO2NPs ( n = 3). (D) TEM images of urine collected from AKI mice after intravenous injection of RuO2NPs . Scale bar: 25nm.

results indicate that RuO2NPs inhibit H2O2-induced MMP dissipation.

Excessive production of intracellular ROS can induce DNA damage, and oxidative byproducts of DNA damage are

Figure7. (A) Body weight of PBS-treated and RuO2NP -treated AKI mice ( n = 5). (B) Kidney body weight groups i -v) ( n = 5). (C) Representative kidneys from groups i -v. (D) H&E staining of kidney tissue from groups i -v. (i) Normal mice, (ii) RuO2NP -treated normal mice, (iii) AKI mice, (iv) PBS-treated AKI mice, (v) RuO2NP -treated AKI mice. Data from four independently replicated experiments. Error bars represent standard deviation. Scale bar: 50 μ m.

Figure8. (A) Creatinine, (B) blood urea nitrogen, (C) creatine kinase, and (D) malondialdehyde levels in normal mice and AKI mice treated with PBS or RuO2NPs ( n = 3).

important biomarkers of oxidative stress. DNA damage induced by ROS includes both nucleotide modifications and DNA strand breaks. γ -H2AX is an indicator of DNA doublestrand breaks, and the immunofluorescence of γ -H2AX can be an important marker of DNA damage. 52As shown in Figure5, intense γ -H2AXfluorescence was observed in immunostained HEK293cells treated with H2O2. The addition of RuO2NPs significantly decreased thefluorescence intensity of γ -H2AX, indicating that the NPs effectively reduce oxidative damage to DNA. The above results clearly show that RuO2NPs can effectively reduce the production of ROS and protect DNA from the damage of oxidative stress.

3.6. Renal Clearance, Pharmacokinetics, and Biodistribution of RuO2NPs. Encouraged by the above results in vitro and the6nm size threshold for efficient renalfiltration, 30the pharmacokinetics, biodistribution, and clearance of

RuO2NPs ( ∼ 2nm) was evaluated in AKI mice. First, 5mg/kg RuO2NPs were intravenously injected into normal mice. After2h postinjection, the AKI was induced by intramuscular injection of50% glycerol into dehydrated mice. Second, as shown in Figure6A, the blood circulation half-life of RuO2NPs in AKI mice was determined to be1.58h by ICP-MS. This indicates that the ultrasmall RuO2NPs have a relatively short blood circulation time. The biodistribution and clearance of RuO2NPs in AKI mice is shown in Figure6B and Figure S7. RuO2NPs gradually accumulated in kidneys within24h of intravenous injection, which may be due to the ultrasmall hydrodynamic diameter of RuO2NPs . High nonspecific accumulation in the lung, spleen, and liver was also detected, however, which could be caused by the serum protein adsorption of RuO2NPs in biologicalfluid in vivo. 53 -55The concentration of RuO2NPs in the organs of

Figure9. Confocal images of DCFH-DA and Hoechst-stained kidney tissues in normal mice and AKI mice treated with PBS or RuO2NPs . Scale bar: 100 μ m.

the mice was assessed for24h after intravenous injection and found to decrease gradually. Most of the RuO2NPs were cleared via the renal system and were excreted by liver metabolization within7days. To further prove the efficient renal clearance of RuO2NPs , we collected and analyzed samples of urine and feces. As shown in Figure6C, timedependent ICP-MS analysis of the Ru concentration exhibited that the majority of the RuO2NPs were detected in the urine within1week. Some of the RuO2NPs were detected in the feces, suggesting that the RuO2NPs were cleared mainly via the renal pathway. TEM images of the urine samples were taken to further verify the clearance pathway. The presence of the NPs in the TEM images further demonstrates their excretion via urine (Figure6D).

- 3.7. Evaluation of AKI Preventing Efficacy. RuO2NPs exhibit effective ROS scavenging in vitro and excellent renal accumulation. In vivo AKI preventing was, therefore, conducted by the intravenous injection of AKI mice with RuO2NPs . AKI mice injected intravenously with PBS served as controls. Changes in body weight were recorded over7days. It was found that PBS-treated AKI mice showed severe weight loss, whereas RuO2NP (5mg/kg)-treated AKI mice gained weight normally (Figure7A). The PBS-treated and RuO2NP -treated AKI mice were sacrificed24h after injection, and results indicate that the kidney weight ratio of PBS-treated AKI mice increased significantly (Figure7B) and the kidneys were significantly enlarged compared with RuO2NP -treated AKI mice. The pictures of the corresponding kidneys are shown in Figure7C. In addition, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of kidney tissue revealed that PBStreated AKI mice had severe renal tubular injury (a marker of kidney damage), whereas renal tubular injury was found to be reduced in RuO2NP -treated AKI mice (Figure7D).

- 3.8. Analysis of Kidney Function after Treatment. To further evaluate the therapeutic effect of the RuO2NPs , we

- analyzed the blood urea nitrogen (BUN), serum creatinine, creatine kinase (CK), and malondialdehyde (MDA) leve, which are clinical indicators of renal excretory function (Figure8A -D). These results showed that PBS-treated AKI mice showed higher levels of BUN, serum creatinine, CK, and MDA, which are signs of renal failure. In addition, these indicators were found to be significantly reduced after the injection of RuO2NPs , indicating that RuO2NP treatment alleviates renal damage in AKI mice.

- 3.9. Confocal Imaging of ROS Production in the Kidneys of AKI Mice. To evaluate whether RuO2NPs can inhibit the excessive production of ROS in the kidney, we stained frozen sections of kidney from the different experimental groups of AKI mice with DCFH-DA (afluorescent probe of ROS) and then them analyzed by laser scanning confocal microscopy. As shown in Figure9, RuO2NPs exhibit a significant inhibitory effect on ROS generation in the kidneys of AKI mice.

- 3.10. Evaluation of the Toxicity of RuO2NPs In Vivo. To assess the renal toxicity of RuO2NPs , kidney sections from renal tubules, collecting ducts, glomeruli, and urethra from AKI mice were collected7days after RuO2NP treatment. The samples were thenfixed with4% paraformaldehyde and stained with H&E. As shown in Figure S8, no obvious tissue damage or inflammatory lesions were detected in the RuO2NP treatment group samples compared to the control group, indicating no obvious side effects. In addition, to verify the biosafety of RuO2NPs , normal mice were injected intravenously with RuO2NPs (5mg/kg), and7days later, the main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, intestine, and brain) were collected for H&E staining (Figure S9). The primary organs of normal mice treated with the NPs displayed no significant damage relative to the control group, indicating that RuO2NPs do not induce adverse side effects in healthy mice. The above results demonstrate the biocompatibility and

low toxicity of RuO2NPs in vivo, making them excellent candidates a as therapeutic agents for the clinical prevention of AKI.


# 4. CONCLUSION


In this study, we demonstrate the novel use of ultrasmall RuO2NPs as an antioxidant. The NPs not only mimic the activities of several enzymes, but also can effectively scavenge free radicals. Although RuO2is a simple and widely used catalyst, the antioxidative activity, biological stability and biocompatibility of ultrasmall RuO2NPs , were demonstrated in HEK293cells for thefirst time. The ROS scavenging and effective inhibition of ROS-induced apoptosis by the NPs reveal the potential of this nanozyme as an effective therapeutic antioxidant in vivo, further proving the outstanding biocompatibility and protective effects of the ultrasmall NPs in AKI mice. Ultrasmall RuO2NPs are a promising agent for the prevention of AKI. In addition, thesefindings indicate this novel nanozyme may be further applied to the prevention of other diseases induced by ROS such as atherosclerosis and neurodegenerative disorders.
